category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Complex Analysis

questiontext.text: If \(f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y)\) is analytic at a point \(z = x + iy\), then the Cauchy-Riemann equations require:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)

The Cauchy-Riemann (C-R) equations are necessary conditions for a complex function \(f = u + iv\) to be analytic (complex differentiable). They state: \(u_x = v_y\) and \(u_y = -v_x\). If these hold and the partial derivatives are continuous, then \(f\) is analytic. The derivative is \(f'(z) = u_x + iv_x = v_y - iu_y\).

answer.text: \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)
\(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)
\(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\)

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), and \(z_0\) is any point inside \(C\), then Cauchy's Integral Formula states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(f(z_0) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i} \displaystyle\oint_C \dfrac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\, dz\)

Cauchy's Integral Formula: for an analytic function \(f\) and a point \(z_0\) inside a simple closed contour \(C\) (traversed counterclockwise): \(f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{z-z_0}\,dz\). More generally, \(f^{(n)}(z_0) = \frac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{n+1}}\,dz\). This shows that analyticity implies infinite differentiability.

questiontext.text: Liouville's Theorem in complex analysis states that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Every bounded entire function is constant

Liouville's Theorem: if \(f\) is entire (analytic on all of \(\mathbb{C}\)) and bounded (i.e., \(|f(z)| \leq M\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{C}\)), then \(f\) is constant. This has a beautiful application: the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (every non-constant polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\)) follows directly from Liouville's theorem.

answer.text: Every analytic function on a bounded domain is constant
Every bounded entire function is constant
Every entire function with a zero is constant

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{z^2}\) at \(z = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 1

\(f(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{z^2} = \dfrac{1}{z^2}\left(1 + z + \dfrac{z^2}{2!} + \cdots\right) = \dfrac{1}{z^2} + \dfrac{1}{z} + \dfrac{1}{2} + \cdots\)

The residue is the coefficient of \(1/z\) in the Laurent expansion, which is \(1\). Alternatively, since \(z=0\) is a pole of order 2: \(\text{Res}_{z=0} f = \lim_{z\to 0} \frac{d}{dz}[z^2 f(z)] = \frac{d}{dz}[e^z]\big|_{z=0} = e^0 = 1\).

questiontext.text: The Maximum Modulus Principle states that if \(f\) is analytic and non-constant on a domain \(D\), then \(|f(z)|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Cannot attain its maximum value at any interior point of \(D\)

The Maximum Modulus Principle: if \(f\) is analytic and non-constant in a domain \(D\), then \(|f|\) has no local maximum inside \(D\). The maximum of \(|f|\) on a closed bounded domain is always attained on the boundary. This is a consequence of the open mapping theorem: a non-constant analytic function maps open sets to open sets.

answer.text: Attains its maximum at the center of \(D\)
Attains its maximum at every point of \(D\)
Cannot attain its maximum value at any interior point of \(D\)

questiontext.text: The Laurent series of \(f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z(z-1)}\) valid for \(0 < |z| < 1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(-\dfrac{1}{z} - 1 - z - z^2 - \cdots\)

Using partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{z(z-1)} = \dfrac{-1}{z} + \dfrac{1}{z-1}\). For \(|z| < 1\): \(\dfrac{1}{z-1} = \dfrac{-1}{1-z} = -(1 + z + z^2 + \cdots)\). So \(f(z) = -\dfrac{1}{z} - 1 - z - z^2 - \cdots\) for \(0 < |z| < 1\). The residue at \(z=0\) is \(-1\).

questiontext.text: A Möbius (linear fractional) transformation \(f(z) = \dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) (with \(ad - bc \neq 0\)) maps:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Circles and lines to circles and lines

Möbius transformations are conformal maps of the Riemann sphere \(\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}\) onto itself. A key property is that they map circles and lines (viewing a line as a circle through \(\infty\)) to circles and lines. They form a group under composition. Every Möbius transformation is a composition of translations, dilations, rotations, and the inversion \(z \mapsto 1/z\).

answer.text: Circles and lines to circles and lines

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Group Theory

questiontext.text: By Lagrange's theorem, if \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\)

Lagrange's Theorem: the order of any subgroup \(H\) of a finite group \(G\) divides the order of \(G\), and \(|G| = |H| \cdot [G:H]\), where \([G:H]\) is the index (number of distinct left cosets of \(H\) in \(G\)). Note: the converse is false in general — not every divisor of \(|G|\) is the order of a subgroup (though Cauchy's theorem guarantees subgroups of prime order).

questiontext.text: A subgroup \(N\) of a group \(G\) is normal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\)

A subgroup \(N \leq G\) is normal (\(N \trianglelefteq G\)) iff \(gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\), equivalently \(gN = Ng\) for all \(g\) (left and right cosets coincide). Normal subgroups are exactly the kernels of group homomorphisms and allow formation of quotient groups \(G/N\). Every subgroup of an abelian group is normal.

questiontext.text: Sylow's First Theorem states: if \(p\) is a prime and \(p^k\) divides \(|G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(p^k\)

Sylow's First Theorem: if \(p^k\) divides \(|G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order \(p^k\). In particular, if \(p^k\) is the highest power of \(p\) dividing \(|G|\), this subgroup is called a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup. Sylow's theorems (there are three) are powerful tools for classifying finite groups. The number of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups divides \(|G|\) and is congruent to 1 mod \(p\).

questiontext.text: Cayley's Theorem states that every group \(G\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A subgroup of a symmetric group \(S_n\)

Cayley's Theorem: every group \(G\) is isomorphic to a subgroup of the symmetric group on \(G\) (i.e., \(\text{Sym}(G)\)). If \(|G| = n\), then \(G\) embeds in \(S_n\). The proof uses the regular representation: associate to each \(g \in G\) the permutation \(\lambda_g: G \to G\) defined by left multiplication \(\lambda_g(x) = gx\).

answer.text: A subgroup of a symmetric group \(S_n\)

questiontext.text: In the symmetric group \(S_3\), the order of the permutation \(\sigma = (1\;2\;3)\) (a 3-cycle) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 3

The order of a permutation is the least common multiple (LCM) of the lengths of its disjoint cycles. The 3-cycle \((1\;2\;3)\) has length 3, so its order is 3. Indeed: \((1\;2\;3)^1 = (1\;2\;3)\), \((1\;2\;3)^2 = (1\;3\;2)\), \((1\;2\;3)^3 = e\) (identity). So \(\sigma^3 = e\), confirming order 3.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Ring Theory

questiontext.text: A commutative ring \(R\) with unity is an integral domain if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) It has no zero divisors

An integral domain is a commutative ring with unity \((1 \neq 0)\) that has no zero divisors, i.e., \(ab = 0\) implies \(a = 0\) or \(b = 0\). Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}\), any field, \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\). Non-examples: \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) (since \(2 \cdot 3 = 0\)). Every finite integral domain is a field.

answer.text: Every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse

name.text: Ring Theory - Prime and Maximal Ideals

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\) with unity, an ideal \(M\) is maximal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(R/M\) is a field

An ideal \(M\) of a commutative ring \(R\) with unity is maximal iff \(R/M\) is a field. An ideal \(P\) is prime iff \(R/P\) is an integral domain. Every maximal ideal is prime (since every field is an integral domain), but not every prime ideal is maximal (e.g., \((0)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\) is prime but not maximal).

questiontext.text: Which of the following is NOT a unique factorization domain (UFD)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\)

In \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), we have \(6 = 2 \times 3 = (1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\), and these give two essentially different factorizations into irreducibles, so it is not a UFD. In contrast, \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a UFD (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic), \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is a UFD (it's a PID), and \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is a UFD (by Gauss's lemma).

questiontext.text: By Eisenstein's criterion, the polynomial \(f(x) = x^4 + 2x^3 + 4x + 2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

Eisenstein's criterion with \(p = 2\): \(p = 2\) divides all coefficients except the leading one (\(2 | 2, 2 | 4, 2 | 2\) but \(2 \nmid 1\)), and \(p^2 = 4 \nmid 2\) (constant term). So by Eisenstein's criterion, \(f(x)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

answer.text: Reducible as a product of two quadratics over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Field Theory and Number Theory

questiontext.text: The number of elements in a finite field \(\mathbb{F}_q\) must satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(q = p^n\) for some prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\)

By the classification of finite fields: a finite field has order \(q = p^n\) where \(p\) is the characteristic (a prime) and \(n \geq 1\). For every prime power \(p^n\), there exists a unique (up to isomorphism) finite field \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) (or \(GF(p^n)\)). Fields of prime order \(p\) are isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: \(q = p^n\) for some prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\)

questiontext.text: The Chinese Remainder Theorem guarantees a unique solution modulo \(mn\) to the system \(x \equiv a \pmod{m}\), \(x \equiv b \pmod{n}\) when:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\)

The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT): if \(\gcd(m,n) = 1\) (i.e., \(m\) and \(n\) are coprime), then for any \(a, b\), the system \(x \equiv a \pmod{m}\), \(x \equiv b \pmod{n}\) has a unique solution modulo \(mn\). This generalizes to any number of pairwise coprime moduli. The CRT is fundamental in number theory and has applications in cryptography and computer arithmetic.

questiontext.text: Euler's totient function \(\phi(12)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 4

\(12 = 2^2 \cdot 3\). Using the formula \(\phi(n) = n \prod_{p|n} (1 - 1/p)\): \(\phi(12) = 12 \cdot (1 - 1/2)(1 - 1/3) = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 4\). The four integers in \(\{1,\ldots,12\}\) coprime to 12 are: 1, 5, 7, 11. Euler's theorem states \(a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}\) when \(\gcd(a,n)=1\).

questiontext.text: Fermat's Little Theorem states that if \(p\) is prime and \(\gcd(a,p) = 1\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}\)

Fermat's Little Theorem: if \(p\) is prime and \(p \nmid a\), then \(a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}\). Equivalently, \(a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}\) for all integers \(a\). This is a special case of Euler's theorem (\(\phi(p) = p-1\) for prime \(p\)). It is widely used in primality testing and modular arithmetic.

questiontext.text: By the Pigeon-hole Principle, if 13 integers are chosen from \(\{1, 2, \ldots, 24\}\), then at least two of them must:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Have a sum equal to 25

Pair the integers: \(\{1,24\}, \{2,23\}, \{3,22\}, \ldots, \{12,13\}\) — 12 pairs that each sum to 25. If we choose 13 integers from \(\{1,\ldots,24\}\), by the Pigeon-hole Principle, at least two must come from the same pair, so their sum equals 25. The Pigeon-hole Principle: if \(n+1\) objects are placed in \(n\) boxes, at least one box contains \(\geq 2\) objects.

answer.text: Have the same remainder when divided by 13

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Topology

questiontext.text: The Heine-Borel theorem states that a subset \(K\) of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(K\) is closed and bounded

The Heine-Borel Theorem (specific to \(\mathbb{R}^n\)): a subset of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact iff it is closed and bounded. This is specific to finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces; in general metric spaces, compactness is equivalent to completeness and total boundedness (not just closed and bounded). For example, the closed unit ball in an infinite-dimensional Banach space is closed and bounded but not compact.

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is connected if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The only subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\)

A space is connected iff it cannot be written as a union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, equivalently, iff the only clopen (simultaneously open and closed) sets are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) itself. Examples of connected spaces: \(\mathbb{R}\), any interval, \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Example of disconnected space: \(\mathbb{Q}\) (every singleton is clopen in the subspace topology).

answer.text: Every open cover has a finite subcover
The only subsets that are both open and closed are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\)
Every continuous function on \(X\) is bounded

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff (T2) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) For any two distinct points, there exist disjoint open sets separating them

A space is Hausdorff (T2) if for any \(x eq y\), there exist open sets \(U i x\) and \(V i y\) with \(U \cap V = \emptyset\). Hausdorff spaces are important because limits of sequences are unique, compact subsets are closed, and continuous functions into Hausdorff spaces are well-behaved. Metric spaces are always Hausdorff.

answer.text: For any two distinct points, there exist disjoint open sets separating them
Every open cover has a finite subcover
Every sequence has a convergent subsequence
The topology is the discrete topology

questiontext.text: By Tychonoff's theorem, the product of compact topological spaces is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Always compact (even for an infinite product)

Tychonoff's Theorem: an arbitrary (possibly infinite) product of compact topological spaces is compact in the product topology. This is one of the most important theorems in topology and is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice. The product topology is the coarsest topology making all projection maps continuous, and a basis consists of products where only finitely many factors differ from the whole space.

answer.text: Compact only if each factor is metrizable
Always compact (even for an infinite product)

questiontext.text: Which of the following implications about topological spaces is TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) T2 (Hausdorff) implies T1

The hierarchy of separation axioms: T0 ⊂ T1 ⊂ T2 (Hausdorff) ⊂ T3 (regular) ⊂ T4 (normal). A T1 space is one where every singleton is closed. A T2 (Hausdorff) space is T1 plus the condition that any two points can be separated by disjoint open sets. Every metric space is T4 (normal), so metric ⟹ Hausdorff ⟹ T1. The reverse implications are generally false.

questiontext.text: A function \(f = u + iv\) is analytic at \(z_0\) if and only if \(u\) and \(v\) satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\partial u/\partial x = \partial v/\partial y\) and \(\partial u/\partial y = -\partial v/\partial x\)

The Cauchy-Riemann equations are necessary and sufficient (with continuous partials) for analyticity. They encode independence of direction for the complex derivative.

answer.text: \(\partial u/\partial x = \partial v/\partial y\) and \(\partial u/\partial y = -\partial v/\partial x\)

questiontext.text: Cauchy's Integral Formula: if \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\) and \(z_0\) is inside \(C\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz\)

This cornerstone result shows analytic functions are determined by boundary values and implies infinite differentiability via \(f^{(n)}(z_0) = \frac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{n+1}}\,dz\).

questiontext.text: Liouville's theorem states that every bounded entire function is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Constant

If \(f\) is entire and bounded, Cauchy's estimate gives \(|f'(z_0)| \leq M/R \to 0\) as \(R\to\infty\), so \(f'=0\). Key application: the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

name.text: Complex Analysis - Laurent Series Principal Part

questiontext.text: In a Laurent series \(\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n(z-z_0)^n\), the principal part consists of:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Terms with negative powers of \((z-z_0)\)

If the principal part has finitely many terms, \(z_0\) is a pole; infinitely many, an essential singularity; no terms, a removable singularity. The residue is the coefficient \(c_{-1}\).

questiontext.text: The Residue Theorem: if \(f\) is analytic inside simple closed contour \(C\) except at poles \(z_1,\ldots,z_n\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \sum_{k=1}^n \text{Res}(f, z_k)\)

Applications include evaluating real integrals \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty R(x)\,dx\) and \(\int_0^{2\pi} R(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\,d\theta\).

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z) = \frac{\sin z}{z}\) has at \(z = 0\) a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Removable singularity

Since \(\lim_{z\to 0}\frac{\sin z}{z} = 1\) exists and is finite, \(z=0\) is a removable singularity. After defining \(f(0)=1\), the function becomes entire.

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is analytic and non-constant on a domain \(D\), then \(|f(z)|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Has no maximum in the interior of \(D\)

The Maximum Modulus Principle: the maximum of \(|f|\) on a closed bounded domain is attained on the boundary. This is a consequence of the Open Mapping Theorem for analytic functions.

answer.text: Attains its maximum at every interior point
Has no maximum in the interior of \(D\)
Attains its maximum at the center of \(D\)

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is continuous on a domain \(D\) and \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz = 0\) for every closed triangle \(C\) in \(D\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(f\) is analytic on \(D\)

Morera's theorem is the converse of Cauchy's theorem. It is used to prove that uniform limits of analytic functions are analytic.

questiontext.text: The Möbius transformation \(f(z) = \frac{az+b}{cz+d}\) (\(ad-bc\neq 0\)) maps:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Circles and lines to circles and lines

Möbius transformations are conformal bijections of \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\), determined by their action on any three points, forming a group under composition isomorphic to \(PSL(2,\mathbb{C})\).

answer.text: Circles and lines to circles and lines
Only the upper half-plane to itself

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is meromorphic inside contour \(C\) with \(Z\) zeros and \(P\) poles, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f'(z)}{f(z)}\,dz = Z - P\)

The Argument Principle counts zeros minus poles via the winding number. Rouché's theorem follows: if \(|g|<|f|\) on \(C\), then \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros inside \(C\).

questiontext.text: If two analytic functions \(f\) and \(g\) on a connected domain \(D\) agree on a set with a limit point in \(D\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f \equiv g\) on all of \(D\)

The Identity Theorem reflects the rigidity of analytic functions — they are completely determined by their values on any set with a limit point. This rigidity is absent in real analysis.

questiontext.text: The Schwarz Lemma: if \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is analytic with \(f(0)=0\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|f(z)|\leq|z|\) and \(|f'(0)|\leq 1\), with equality iff \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}z\)

The Schwarz Lemma is proved by applying the Maximum Modulus Principle to \(g(z)=f(z)/z\). It classifies automorphisms of the unit disk as Möbius transformations.

questiontext.text: If \(f=u+iv\) is analytic, then \(u\) and \(v\) satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\Delta u=0\) and \(\Delta v=0\) (both harmonic)

Real and imaginary parts of analytic functions are harmonic. Conversely, on simply connected domains, every harmonic \(u\) has a harmonic conjugate \(v\) (unique up to constant) making \(u+iv\) analytic.

questiontext.text: Picard's Little Theorem: a non-constant entire function omits:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) At most one complex value

For example, \(e^z\) omits only 0. If a function omits two values it must be constant. Picard's Great Theorem strengthens this near essential singularities.

answer.text: All values on the unit circle

name.text: Complex Analysis - Order of Entire Function

questiontext.text: The order of the entire function \(e^z\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 1

Order \(\rho = \limsup_{r\to\infty}\frac{\log\log M(r)}{\log r}\). For \(e^z\): \(M(r)=e^r\), so \(\log\log M(r)=\log r\), giving \(\rho=1\). By Hadamard's theorem, the genus and order govern the product representation of entire functions.

questiontext.text: Rouché's theorem: if \(|g(z)|<|f(z)|\) on contour \(C\), then inside \(C\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros

This follows from the Argument Principle. It is used to locate zeros of polynomials: e.g., \(f(z)=z^n\) dominates on \(|z|=R\) for large \(R\), so \(f+g\) has \(n\) zeros inside a large disk.

answer.text: \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros

questiontext.text: The zeros of a non-constant analytic function on a domain \(D\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Isolated

If zeros accumulated they would form a set with a limit point, forcing \(f\equiv 0\) by the Identity Theorem. Each zero \(z_0\) has finite order \(m\): \(f(z)=(z-z_0)^m g(z)\) with \(g(z_0)\neq 0\).

questiontext.text: If \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), Lagrange's theorem states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\)

\(|G|=|H|\cdot[G:H]\) where \([G:H]\) is the index. The order of every element divides \(|G|\). If \(|G|=p\) (prime), \(G\) is cyclic.

questiontext.text: A subgroup \(N\leq G\) is normal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(gNg^{-1}=N\) for all \(g\in G\)

Equivalently: left and right cosets coincide; \(N\) is a kernel of a homomorphism; \(N\) is a union of conjugacy classes. Every subgroup of an abelian group and every subgroup of index 2 is normal.

questiontext.text: If \(\phi:G\to H\) is a group homomorphism, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(G/\ker\phi \cong \phi(G)\)

The First Isomorphism Theorem. The Second: \(H/(H\cap N)\cong HN/N\). The Third: \((G/N)/(M/N)\cong G/M\). These are the fundamental tools for group calculations.

questiontext.text: If \(|G|=p^am\) with \(\gcd(p,m)=1\), Sylow I guarantees existence of:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) A subgroup of order \(p^a\)

Sylow II: all Sylow \(p\)-subgroups are conjugate. Sylow III: \(n_p\equiv 1\pmod p\) and \(n_p\mid m\). These theorems are fundamental for classifying groups of given order.

questiontext.text: Every subgroup of a cyclic group is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Cyclic

For \(\mathbb{Z}_n\), subgroups are \(\langle d\rangle\) for each \(d\mid n\), with \(|\langle d\rangle|=n/d\). The number of subgroups equals the number of divisors of \(n\).

questiontext.text: A group \(G\) is simple if its only normal subgroups are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\{e\}\) and \(G\)

Simple groups are the building blocks of group theory. Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}_p\), \(A_n\) for \(n\geq 5\). The Classification of Finite Simple Groups (CFSG) lists all finite simple groups.

questiontext.text: The alternating group \(A_n\subseteq S_n\) consists of:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) All even permutations

\(|A_n|=n!/2\). For \(n\geq 5\), \(A_n\) is simple and non-abelian, which is why the general degree-5 polynomial is unsolvable by radicals (Galois theory).

questiontext.text: For a group \(G\) acting on set \(X\), the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem gives:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(|\text{Orb}(x)|\cdot|\text{Stab}(x)|=|G|\)

Used in Burnside's lemma for counting orbits: \(|\text{orbits}|=\frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}|X^g|\). This generalises Lagrange's theorem.

questiontext.text: The class equation of a finite group \(G\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum_i[G:C_G(x_i)]\)

Key application: if \(|G|=p^n\), then \(p\mid|Z(G)|\), so \(Z(G)\neq\{e\}\). This implies groups of order \(p^2\) are abelian.

name.text: Group Theory - Finitely Generated Abelian Groups

questiontext.text: Every finitely generated abelian group is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\mathbb{Z}^r\oplus\mathbb{Z}_{d_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{Z}_{d_k}\) with \(d_1\mid\cdots\mid d_k\)

This is the fundamental theorem of finitely generated abelian groups, with \(r\) the rank (free part) and the \(d_i\) the invariant factors (torsion part), unique up to reordering.

questiontext.text: A group \(G\) is solvable if it has a subnormal series with each quotient \(G_i/G_{i+1}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Abelian

A polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is solvable. \(S_n\) is solvable for \(n\leq 4\) but not \(n\geq 5\). Feit-Thompson: every odd-order group is solvable.

questiontext.text: The dihedral group \(D_n\) of order \(2n\) is generated by \(r\) and \(s\) satisfying:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(r^n=s^2=e\) and \(srs^{-1}=r^{-1}\)

\(D_n\) is the symmetry group of a regular \(n\)-gon, with \(n\) rotations and \(n\) reflections. It is non-abelian for \(n\geq 3\) and is a semidirect product \(\mathbb{Z}_n\rtimes\mathbb{Z}_2\).

name.text: Ring Theory - Prime vs Maximal Ideals

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\), an ideal \(I\) is prime iff \(R/I\) is an integral domain, and maximal iff \(R/I\) is a field. Which implication always holds?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Maximal \(\Rightarrow\) prime

Every field is an integral domain, so every maximal ideal is prime. The converse fails: in \(\mathbb{Z}\), \((0)\) is prime (since \(\mathbb{Z}\) is an integral domain) but not maximal (since \((0)\subsetneq(2)\subsetneq\mathbb{Z}\)).

answer.text: They are equivalent in all rings

name.text: Ring Theory - UFD implies PID is false

questiontext.text: Which of the following is a UFD but NOT a PID?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\)

\(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is a UFD (by Gauss's lemma, since \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a UFD), but the ideal \((2,x)=\{2f+xg:f,g\in\mathbb{Z}[x]\}\) is not principal (no single polynomial generates both 2 and \(x\)). This shows the chain ED\(\Rightarrow\)PID\(\Rightarrow\)UFD is strict.

questiontext.text: By Eisenstein's criterion with \(p=2\), the polynomial \(f(x)=x^4+2x^3+4x+6\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

Check: \(p=2\) does not divide the leading coefficient \(1\); \(2\mid 2,4,6\) (all non-leading coefficients); \(4\nmid 6\) (the constant term). So Eisenstein applies and \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

questiontext.text: If \(m_1,\ldots,m_k\) are pairwise coprime, then \(\mathbb{Z}/(m_1\cdots m_k)\cong\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\mathbb{Z}/m_1\times\cdots\times\mathbb{Z}/m_k\)

CRT gives a unique solution modulo \(m_1\cdots m_k\) to any simultaneous congruences. Applied in RSA, fast arithmetic, and polynomial interpolation.

questiontext.text: The nilradical of a commutative ring \(R\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)

The nilradical \(\sqrt{(0)}=\bigcap_{\mathfrak{p}\text{ prime}}\mathfrak{p}\). The quotient \(R/\sqrt{(0)}\) is reduced (no nonzero nilpotents). In \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), the nilradical is generated by the radical of \(n\).

answer.text: The intersection of all maximal ideals
The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)
The set of all units of \(R\)

questiontext.text: The degree \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 4

By the tower law: \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2):\mathbb{Q}]=2\) and \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2,\sqrt3):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2)]=2\) (since \(\sqrt3\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2)\)). Product gives 4. A basis is \(\{1,\sqrt2,\sqrt3,\sqrt6\}\).

name.text: Field Theory - Galois Group of Biquadratic

questiontext.text: The Galois group \(\text{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2,\sqrt3)/\mathbb{Q})\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_2\) (Klein four-group)

The four automorphisms are \(\text{id}\), \(\sqrt2\mapsto-\sqrt2\), \(\sqrt3\mapsto-\sqrt3\), and both. Each has order 2 and the group is abelian of order 4, isomorphic to the Klein four-group \(V_4\).

name.text: Field Theory - Finite Fields Multiplicative Group

questiontext.text: For a finite field \(\mathbb{F}_q\), the multiplicative group \(\mathbb{F}_q^*\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Cyclic of order \(q-1\)

A generator is called a primitive root. For example, \(\mathbb{F}_7^*\cong\mathbb{Z}_6\), generated by 3. This cyclicity is fundamental in discrete logarithm-based cryptography.

questiontext.text: The degree of the splitting field of \(x^3-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 6

Roots are \(\sqrt[3]2,\,\omega\sqrt[3]2,\,\omega^2\sqrt[3]2\) where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\). The splitting field is \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]2,\omega)\) with \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]2):\mathbb{Q}]=3\) and \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]2,\omega):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]2)]=2\), giving degree 6. The Galois group is \(S_3\).

questiontext.text: Euler's theorem: if \(\gcd(a,n)=1\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(a^{\phi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n\)

This generalises Fermat's little theorem (\(n=p\) prime). RSA uses this: \(m^{ed}\equiv m\pmod n\) when \(ed\equiv1\pmod{\phi(n)}\). The totient \(\phi(n)=n\prod_{p\mid n}(1-1/p)\).

questiontext.text: Euler's criterion for the Legendre symbol states \(\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\equiv\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(a^{(p-1)/2}\pmod p\)

This follows from Fermat's little theorem. The Quadratic Reciprocity Law (Gauss): \(\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)=(-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2}\cdot\frac{q-1}{2}}\) for distinct odd primes \(p,q\).

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is compact if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Every open cover has a finite subcover

Compact Hausdorff spaces are normal. Continuous images of compact spaces are compact. A closed subset of compact is compact; a compact subset of Hausdorff is closed. Tychonoff: products of compact spaces are compact.

answer.text: Every open cover has a finite subcover
Every sequence has a Cauchy subsequence
\(X\) is closed and bounded in some ambient space

questiontext.text: A topological space is Hausdorff (T2) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Any two distinct points have disjoint open neighbourhoods

Hausdorff spaces have unique limits for sequences and nets. Compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed. The hierarchy: T1\(\subset\)T2\(\subset\)T3\(\subset\)T4. Metric spaces are T4 (normal).

answer.text: It has a countable dense subset
Any two distinct points have disjoint open neighbourhoods
Every open cover has a finite subcover

questiontext.text: Urysohn's Lemma: in a normal space, for disjoint closed sets \(A\) and \(B\), there exists continuous \(f:X\to[0,1]\) with:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(f(A)=\{0\}\) and \(f(B)=\{1\}\)

This characterises normal (T4) spaces. It is used to prove the Tietze Extension Theorem and Urysohn's Metrization Theorem.

questiontext.text: Which statement about path-connectedness and connectedness is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Path-connected \(\Rightarrow\) connected, but not conversely

The topologist's sine curve \(\{(x,\sin(1/x)):x>0\}\cup\{(0,y):-1\leq y\leq 1\}\) is connected but not path-connected. For open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the two notions coincide.

answer.text: They are equivalent in all spaces

questiontext.text: The fundamental group \(\pi_1(S^1)\) of the circle is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\mathbb{Z}\)

The generator is one full loop around the circle; the integer counts the winding number. \(\pi_1(S^n)=0\) for \(n\geq2\) (simply connected). The torus has \(\pi_1(T^2)=\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}\).

questiontext.text: Tychonoff's theorem states that an arbitrary product of compact spaces is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Compact in the product topology

Tychonoff's theorem is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice. The product topology is the coarsest making all projections continuous — sequences converge coordinatewise.

questiontext.text: The quotient space \([0,1]/\{0,1\}\) (endpoints identified) is homeomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(S^1\)

Identifying the two endpoints of \([0,1]\) glues them into a loop, topologically a circle. Similarly, identifying opposite edges of \([0,1]^2\) gives the torus.

questiontext.text: \(X\times Y\) is connected if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Both \(X\) and \(Y\) are connected

Proof: the "slices" \(X\times\{y_0\}\cong X\) and \(\{x\}\times Y\cong Y\) are connected and share a point, so their union is connected; every point lies in such a union sharing a common point.

answer.text: At least one of \(X\),\(Y\) is connected

questiontext.text: Two spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic if there is a bijection \(f:X\to Y\) that is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Continuous with a continuous inverse

A bijective continuous map whose inverse is also continuous. Bijective continuous alone is insufficient (e.g., the map \([0,1)\to S^1\) winding once). Homeomorphic spaces share all topological invariants: compactness, connectedness, fundamental group, etc.

answer.text: A bijective continuous map (no condition on inverse)

name.text: Complex Analysis - Residue at Simple Pole

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z)=\frac{g(z)}{h(z)}\) at a simple zero \(z_0\) of \(h\) (where \(g(z_0)\neq0\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{g(z_0)}{h'(z_0)}\)

For a simple pole at \(z_0\): \(\text{Res}(f,z_0)=\lim_{z\to z_0}(z-z_0)f(z)=g(z_0)/h'(z_0)\). For a pole of order \(m\): \(\text{Res}=\frac{1}{(m-1)!}\lim_{z\to z_0}\frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}}[(z-z_0)^m f(z)]\).

questiontext.text: A commutative ring \(R\) is an integral domain if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(R\) has no zero-divisors (i.e., \(ab=0\Rightarrow a=0\) or \(b=0\))

Equivalently, the cancellation law holds: \(ab=ac\) and \(a\neq0\Rightarrow b=c\). Every field is an integral domain. Every finite integral domain is a field (Wedderburn). The prototype is \(\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: Every element has a multiplicative inverse

questiontext.text: Cauchy's theorem for groups states: if \(p\) is a prime dividing \(|G|\), then \(G\) has:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) An element of order \(p\)

Cauchy's theorem is a partial converse to Lagrange's theorem (for prime divisors). It follows from Sylow I (the case of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups). Combined with Sylow theory, it helps classify groups of small order.

question.single: truetrueabc A subgroup of order \(p^2\) An element of order \(p\) A normal subgroup of index \(p\) A unique subgroup of order \(p\)

name.text: Complex Analysis - Contour Integration Real Integral

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{dx}{1+x^2}\) by residues, the poles of \(\frac{1}{1+z^2}\) in the upper half-plane are at:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(z=i\)

\(1+z^2=(z-i)(z+i)\). Only \(z=i\) is in the upper half-plane. \(\text{Res}(f,i)=\frac{1}{2i}\). By the Residue Theorem: \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty\frac{dx}{1+x^2}=2\pi i\cdot\frac{1}{2i}=\pi\). This equals \([\arctan x]_{-\infty}^\infty=\pi\), confirming the result.

questiontext.text: If \(F\subseteq K\subseteq L\) are fields, the Tower Law states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \([L:F]=[L:K][K:F]\)

The Tower Law is fundamental in computing degrees of field extensions. Example: \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2,\sqrt3):\mathbb{Q}]=[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2,\sqrt3):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2)]\cdot[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2):\mathbb{Q}]=2\cdot2=4\).

questiontext.text: The Jacobson radical \(J(R)\) of a ring \(R\) is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The intersection of all maximal left ideals of \(R\)

The Jacobson radical consists of elements that annihilate all simple modules. For commutative rings, \(J(R)\) is the intersection of all maximal ideals. A ring with \(J(R)=0\) is called semisimple (Jacobson semisimple). For Artinian rings, \(J(R)\) is the unique maximal nilpotent ideal.

answer.text: The set of all nilpotent elements
The intersection of all maximal left ideals of \(R\)
The intersection of all prime ideals

questiontext.text: A continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) A homeomorphism

If \(f:X\to Y\) is a continuous bijection, \(X\) is compact, and \(Y\) is Hausdorff, then \(f\) is a homeomorphism because \(f\) is a closed map (compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed, and continuous images of compact sets are compact). This is a standard and useful theorem.

answer.text: Not necessarily continuous in the other direction

questiontext.text: The free group \(F_n\) on \(n\geq2\) generators is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Non-abelian and infinite

Free groups have no relations other than group axioms. \(F_1\cong\mathbb{Z}\) (abelian), but \(F_n\) for \(n\geq2\) is non-abelian and infinite. Every group is a quotient of a free group (every group has a presentation). Free groups are the building blocks of combinatorial group theory.

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass product formula for \(\sin(\pi z)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\pi z\prod_{n=1}^\infty\left(1-\frac{z^2}{n^2}\right)\)

The Weierstrass factorisation theorem: every entire function can be written as a product involving its zeros. For \(\sin\pi z\), zeros are at all integers, giving this product. Setting \(z=1/2\) yields Wallis's product \(\pi/2=\prod_{n=1}^\infty\frac{4n^2}{4n^2-1}\).

questiontext.text: A field \(F\) is algebraically closed if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Every non-constant polynomial in \(F[x]\) has a root in \(F\)

The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra states \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed (proved using Liouville's theorem). Algebraically closed fields have no proper algebraic extensions. Every field has an algebraic closure (unique up to isomorphism, by Zorn's Lemma and AC).

answer.text: Every non-constant polynomial in \(F[x]\) has a root in \(F\)

name.text: Ring Theory - Polynomial Rings over Fields

questiontext.text: If \(F\) is a field, then \(F[x]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A Euclidean domain (hence a PID and UFD)

\(F[x]\) has a division algorithm: for \(f,g\in F[x]\) with \(g\neq0\), there exist unique \(q,r\) with \(f=qg+r\) and \(\deg r<\deg g\). This makes \(F[x]\) Euclidean with norm \(\deg\). Irreducibles in \(F[x]\) are the irreducible polynomials; every ideal is \((p(x))\) for some polynomial.

answer.text: A Euclidean domain (hence a PID and UFD)
A UFD but not a PID
An integral domain but not a UFD

questiontext.text: The one-point compactification of \(\mathbb{R}\) (adding a point \(\infty\)) is homeomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(S^1\)

The one-point compactification of \(\mathbb{R}\) adds a single point \(\infty\) and is homeomorphic to the circle \(S^1\) (via stereographic projection). Similarly, the one-point compactification of \(\mathbb{R}^2\) is \(S^2\), and of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is \(S^n\). This is used in complex analysis (Riemann sphere \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}=\mathbb{C}\cup\{\infty\}\)).

questiontext.text: Cauchy's Theorem states: if \(f\) is analytic on and inside a simple closed contour \(C\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz = 0\)

Cauchy's theorem is the foundation of complex integration. It holds because analytic functions have no singularities. The converse (Morera's theorem) also holds. The theorem extends to multiply connected regions.

name.text: Complex Analysis - Analytic Functions are Infinitely Differentiable

questiontext.text: A function that is complex differentiable in a neighborhood of \(z_0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Infinitely differentiable (and equal to its Taylor series) near \(z_0\)

This is a fundamental difference from real analysis: a complex differentiable function is automatically analytic (equals its Taylor series) in a neighborhood of each point. Real differentiability does not imply smoothness (e.g., \(f(x)=x|x|\) is differentiable but not twice differentiable at 0).

answer.text: Infinitely differentiable (and equal to its Taylor series) near \(z_0\)

name.text: Complex Analysis - Fundamental Theorem of Algebra via Liouville

questiontext.text: Every non-constant polynomial \(p(z)\) of degree \(n\geq 1\) over \(\mathbb{C}\) has exactly:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(n\) roots in \(\mathbb{C}\) (counted with multiplicity)

The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed. Proof via Liouville: if \(p\) had no root, \(1/p\) would be a bounded entire function, hence constant. By induction (or the factorisation theorem), \(p\) has exactly \(n\) roots counted with multiplicity.

questiontext.text: The Riemann Mapping Theorem states: every simply connected proper open subset \(U\subsetneq\mathbb{C}\) is biholomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The open unit disk \(\mathbb{D}\)

This remarkable theorem shows all simply connected proper domains are conformally equivalent to \(\mathbb{D}\). The mapping is unique if we require it to fix a given point and have positive derivative there. The theorem fails for \(\mathbb{C}\) itself (by Liouville) and for non-simply connected domains.

questiontext.text: The principal branch of \(\log z\) is defined on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty,0]\) with:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\log z = \ln|z| + i\arg z\) where \(\arg z\in(-\pi,\pi]\)

The logarithm is multivalued: \(\log z = \ln|z|+i(\arg z+2\pi k)\) for \(k\in\mathbb{Z}\). The principal branch uses \(\text{Arg}\,z\in(-\pi,\pi]\), with branch cut on the negative real axis. Branch cuts are needed whenever a function is multivalued (also \(z^\alpha\), \(\arcsin z\), etc.).

name.text: Group Theory - Abelian Groups of Order \(p^2\)

questiontext.text: Every group of order \(p^2\) (where \(p\) is prime) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Abelian

Proof: the centre \(Z(G)\) has order \(p\) or \(p^2\) (by the class equation). If \(|Z(G)|=p^2\), done. If \(|Z(G)|=p\), then \(G/Z(G)\) has order \(p\) hence is cyclic, which forces \(G\) to be abelian (a standard lemma: \(G/Z(G)\) cyclic \(\Rightarrow G\) abelian). So \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_{p^2}\) or \(\mathbb{Z}_p\times\mathbb{Z}_p\).

questiontext.text: The commutator subgroup (derived subgroup) \([G,G]\) of \(G\) is the smallest normal subgroup \(N\) such that \(G/N\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Abelian

The abelianisation of \(G\) is \(G^{ab}=G/[G,G]\). Commutators \([a,b]=aba^{-1}b^{-1}\) generate \([G,G]\). For \(S_3\): \([S_3,S_3]=A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\), and \(S_3/A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\). A group is abelian iff \([G,G]=\{e\}\). Solvability is characterised by the derived series reaching \(\{e\}\).

questiontext.text: An element \(a\in\mathbb{Z}_n\) is a unit (has a multiplicative inverse) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\gcd(a,n)=1\)

The units of \(\mathbb{Z}_n\) form the group \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*\) of order \(\phi(n)\) (Euler's totient). When \(n=p\) is prime, every nonzero element is a unit, making \(\mathbb{Z}_p\) a field. The structure of \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*\) is known for all \(n\).

questiontext.text: Two spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) are homotopy equivalent if there exist continuous maps \(f:X\to Y\) and \(g:Y\to X\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(g\circ f\simeq\text{id}_X\) and \(f\circ g\simeq\text{id}_Y\)

Homotopy equivalence is weaker than homeomorphism. For example, \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is homotopy equivalent to a point (contractible). The circle \(S^1\) is homotopy equivalent to the punctured plane \(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\). Homotopy-equivalent spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups and homology groups.

questiontext.text: The universal cover of \(S^1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\mathbb{R}\)

The map \(p:\mathbb{R}\to S^1\), \(p(t)=e^{2\pi it}\) is a covering map. \(\mathbb{R}\) is simply connected (contractible), making it the universal cover. The deck transformations are \(t\mapsto t+n\) for \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\), forming a group isomorphic to \(\pi_1(S^1)=\mathbb{Z}\).

questiontext.text: The tensor product \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\otimes_\mathbb{Z}\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(0\) (the zero module)

Since \(\gcd(2,3)=1\), there exist integers \(a,b\) with \(2a+3b=1\). For any \(x\otimes y\): \(x\otimes y = x\otimes(2a+3b)y = x\otimes 2ay + x\otimes 3by = 2ax\otimes y + x\otimes 3by\). Since \(2\bar1=0\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/2\) and \(3\bar1=0\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/3\), this equals 0. More generally, \(\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes\mathbb{Z}/n\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\).

questiontext.text: In the Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory, for a Galois extension \(L/K\), there is a bijection between:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Subgroups of \(\text{Gal}(L/K)\) and intermediate fields \(K\subseteq F\subseteq L\)

The Galois correspondence: \(H\leq G=\text{Gal}(L/K)\mapsto L^H=\{x\in L: \sigma(x)=x\,\forall\sigma\in H\}\), and \(F\mapsto\text{Gal}(L/F)\). This is order-reversing. Normal subgroups correspond to Galois intermediate extensions. \([L:F]=|H|\) and \([F:K]=[G:H]\).

answer.text: Subgroups of \(\text{Gal}(L/K)\) and intermediate fields \(K\subseteq F\subseteq L\)
Prime ideals and roots of polynomials

questiontext.text: Wilson's theorem states that a positive integer \(n>1\) is prime if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \((n-1)!\equiv -1\pmod n\)

Wilson's theorem: \(p\) is prime iff \((p-1)!\equiv-1\pmod p\). Proof: in \(\mathbb{F}_p^*\), elements pair with their inverses; only \(\pm1\) are self-inverse, giving \((p-1)!\equiv-1\). While theoretically elegant, Wilson's theorem is impractical for primality testing.

questiontext.text: The Taylor series of \(\frac{1}{1-z}\) around \(z=0\) converges for:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(|z|<1\)

\(\frac{1}{1-z}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty z^n\) for \(|z|<1\). The radius of convergence is 1 (determined by the nearest singularity at \(z=1\)). This geometric series is the prototypical power series and is used to derive many other series expansions.

questiontext.text: The normaliser of a subgroup \(H\) in \(G\) is \(N_G(H)=\{g\in G:gHg^{-1}=H\}\). Which statement is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(H\trianglelefteq N_G(H)\) and \(N_G(H)\) is the largest subgroup in which \(H\) is normal

The normaliser \(N_G(H)\) is always a subgroup containing \(H\), and \(H\) is normal in \(N_G(H)\). If \(N_G(H)=G\), then \(H\) is normal in \(G\). The number of conjugates of \(H\) is \([G:N_G(H)]\).

answer.text: \(H\trianglelefteq N_G(H)\) and \(N_G(H)\) is the largest subgroup in which \(H\) is normal

questiontext.text: The Euler characteristic \(\chi\) of a CW complex satisfies \(\chi=V-E+F\) for surfaces. For the sphere \(S^2\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\chi(S^2)=2\)

For the sphere: \(V=2, E=0, F=0\) (two 0-cells and one 2-cell) gives \(\chi=2-0+1=2\). For the torus: \(\chi=0\). For genus-\(g\) surface: \(\chi=2-2g\). The Euler characteristic is a topological invariant.

questiontext.text: A ring \(R\) is Noetherian if it satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The ascending chain condition on ideals (ACC)

Noetherian rings: every ascending chain of ideals \(I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq\cdots\) stabilises. Equivalently, every ideal is finitely generated. By the Hilbert Basis Theorem, if \(R\) is Noetherian then \(R[x]\) is Noetherian. \(\mathbb{Z}\) and every field are Noetherian.

answer.text: The ascending chain condition on ideals (ACC)
The descending chain condition on ideals (DCC)

questiontext.text: An Artinian ring satisfies the descending chain condition. Every Artinian integral domain is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) A field

In an Artinian integral domain, for any nonzero \(a\), the descending chain \((a)\supseteq(a^2)\supseteq\cdots\) stabilises at some \((a^n)=(a^{n+1})\), giving \(a^n=a^{n+1}b\), so \(1=ab\) (since no zero-divisors), making \(a\) a unit. Thus every nonzero element is a unit, making it a field.

questiontext.text: The Seifert-van Kampen theorem computes the fundamental group of \(X=U\cup V\) (with \(U,V,U\cap V\) path-connected) as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The free product \(\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)\) (amalgamated free product)

Van Kampen's theorem: \(\pi_1(X)\cong\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)\), the pushout in the category of groups. Used to compute \(\pi_1(S^2)=0\) (decompose as two disks), \(\pi_1(\text{torus})=\mathbb{Z}*\mathbb{Z}/[a,b]=\mathbb{Z}^2\), etc.

answer.text: The free product \(\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)\) (amalgamated free product)

questiontext.text: The winding number of a closed curve \(\gamma\) around a point \(z_0\) not on \(\gamma\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma \frac{dz}{z-z_0}\)

The winding number \(n(\gamma,z_0)\in\mathbb{Z}\) counts (with orientation) how many times \(\gamma\) winds around \(z_0\). It is constant on connected components of \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\gamma\) and is used in the general statement of Cauchy's theorem and the Argument Principle.

questiontext.text: Burnside's Lemma states that the number of distinct orbits of a group \(G\) acting on a finite set \(X\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}|X^g|\)

Here \(X^g=\{x\in X:gx=x\}\) is the set of fixed points of \(g\). This is used to count combinatorial objects "up to symmetry", e.g., necklaces, graph colourings. It is also called the Cauchy-Frobenius lemma.

questiontext.text: The Hilbert Basis Theorem states: if \(R\) is Noetherian, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(R[x]\) is Noetherian

Hilbert's Basis Theorem: the polynomial ring over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian. By induction, \(R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) is Noetherian whenever \(R\) is. This implies algebraic varieties are defined by finitely many equations, foundational in algebraic geometry.

questiontext.text: Brouwer's Fixed Point Theorem states: every continuous map \(f:D^n\to D^n\) (closed unit ball):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Has at least one fixed point

The theorem holds for any compact convex set. In 1D it follows from IVT. It is used to prove Nash equilibria in game theory. The proof uses homology theory or the non-existence of a retraction from \(D^n\) to \(S^{n-1}\).

answer.text: Has at least one fixed point
Has no fixed points on the boundary

name.text: Field Theory - Characteristic of a Field

questiontext.text: The characteristic of a field \(F\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 0 or a prime \(p\)

The characteristic is the smallest positive integer \(n\) with \(n\cdot 1_F=0\), or 0 if none exists. In a field, if \(\text{char}(F)=n>0\), then \(n\) must be prime (if \(n=ab\), then \((a\cdot 1)(b\cdot 1)=0\) forces \(a\cdot 1=0\) or \(b\cdot 1=0\) since fields have no zero-divisors). \(\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}\) have char 0; \(\mathbb{F}_p\) has char \(p\).

questiontext.text: The Poisson integral formula expresses a harmonic function \(u\) inside the disk \(|z|

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(u(r,\theta)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\frac{R^2-r^2}{R^2-2Rr\cos(\theta-\phi)+r^2}u(R,\phi)\,d\phi\)

The Poisson kernel \(P_r(\theta)=\frac{R^2-r^2}{R^2-2Rr\cos\theta+r^2}\) is the real part of the Cauchy kernel. It solves the Dirichlet problem: given continuous boundary data, it constructs the unique harmonic extension to the disk.

name.text: Group Theory - Centre of a Group

questiontext.text: The centre \(Z(G)=\{g\in G:gx=xg\;\forall x\in G\}\). For a non-abelian simple group:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(Z(G)=\{e\}\)

The centre is always a normal subgroup. For a simple group, the only normal subgroups are \(\{e\}\) and \(G\). Since \(Z(G)\neq G\) (otherwise \(G\) would be abelian hence not simple unless trivial), we must have \(Z(G)=\{e\}\). Examples: \(A_5\) has trivial centre.

questiontext.text: The fundamental group \(\pi_1(T^2)\) of the torus is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}\)

The torus \(T^2=S^1\times S^1\) has \(\pi_1(T^2)=\pi_1(S^1)\times\pi_1(S^1)=\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}\). The two generators are the two independent loops. The torus can also be described as \([0,1]^2\) with opposite edges identified, with cell structure giving presentation \(\langle a,b\mid aba^{-1}b^{-1}=1\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}^2\).

name.text: Ring Theory - Gauss's Lemma for UFDs

questiontext.text: If \(R\) is a UFD, then the polynomial ring \(R[x]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Also a UFD

By Gauss's Lemma: product of primitive polynomials is primitive. The irreducibles in \(R[x]\) are: irreducibles of \(R\) (viewed as constants) and primitive polynomials irreducible in \(F[x]\) where \(F\) is the fraction field of \(R\). So \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\), \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) are all UFDs.

questiontext.text: The Schwarz-Pick lemma states that a holomorphic map \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is a contraction with respect to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The Poincaré (hyperbolic) metric on \(\mathbb{D}\)

The Poincaré metric \(ds = \frac{2|dz|}{1-|z|^2}\) makes \(\mathbb{D}\) a model of the hyperbolic plane. The Schwarz-Pick lemma: holomorphic self-maps of \(\mathbb{D}\) are non-expansive; isometries are exactly the Möbius automorphisms \(z\mapsto e^{i\theta}\frac{z-a}{1-\bar az}\).

questiontext.text: Fermat's Little Theorem states: if \(p\) is prime and \(\gcd(a,p)=1\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(a^{p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p\)

Equivalently, \(a^p\equiv a\pmod p\) for all \(a\). This follows from Lagrange's theorem: the multiplicative group \(\mathbb{F}_p^*\) has order \(p-1\), so every element satisfies \(x^{p-1}=1\). Used in primality tests (Fermat test, Miller-Rabin).

name.text: Topology - Normal Spaces and Tietze Extension

questiontext.text: The Tietze Extension Theorem states: in a normal space \(X\), any continuous \(f:A\to[a,b]\) defined on a closed set \(A\) can be:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Extended to a continuous \(F:X\to[a,b]\)

The Tietze Extension Theorem is equivalent to Urysohn's Lemma (both characterise normal spaces). It shows normal spaces are "nice" enough to extend real-valued continuous functions. This is used in proving partitions of unity exist in paracompact spaces.

answer.text: Extended but may take values outside \([a,b]\)

questiontext.text: The localization \(S^{-1}R\) of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(S\) (with \(0\notin S\)) is formed by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Formally inverting all elements of \(S\): elements are fractions \(r/s\) with \(r\in R\), \(s\in S\)

Localization generalises how \(\mathbb{Z}\) embeds in \(\mathbb{Q}\) (inverting all nonzero integers). For a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\), \(R_\mathfrak{p}=S^{-1}R\) with \(S=R\setminus\mathfrak{p}\) is a local ring (unique maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{p}R_\mathfrak{p}\)). Fundamental in algebraic geometry and commutative algebra.

answer.text: Quotienting \(R\) by the ideal generated by \(S\)
Formally inverting all elements of \(S\): elements are fractions \(r/s\) with \(r\in R\), \(s\in S\)

questiontext.text: A representation of a group \(G\) is a homomorphism \(\rho:G\to GL(V)\) for some vector space \(V\). The degree (dimension) of the representation is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\dim V\)

Representations encode group actions on vector spaces. Maschke's theorem: for finite groups over \(\mathbb{C}\) (or fields with \(\text{char}\nmid|G|\)), every representation decomposes as a direct sum of irreducible representations. The number of irreducible representations equals the number of conjugacy classes.

questiontext.text: The Mittag-Leffler theorem states that given a sequence of distinct points \(\{z_n\}\to\infty\) and principal parts \(P_n(1/(z-z_n))\), there exists a meromorphic function on \(\mathbb{C}\) with:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Exactly the prescribed poles \(z_n\) with prescribed principal parts

Mittag-Leffler generalises partial fractions to infinite sums. Any meromorphic function can be written as a sum of its principal parts (with convergence factors) plus an entire function. This is the additive analogue of the Weierstrass product theorem (for zeros).

answer.text: Exactly the prescribed poles \(z_n\) with prescribed principal parts

questiontext.text: An algebraic extension \(L/K\) is separable if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Every element of \(L\) has a separable minimal polynomial over \(K\) (no repeated roots)

A polynomial is separable iff it has no repeated roots (in the algebraic closure), equivalently \(\gcd(f,f')=1\). All algebraic extensions of \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{R}\), \(\mathbb{C}\), and any finite field are separable. Extensions of fields of characteristic \(p\) may not be separable.

answer.text: Every element of \(L\) has a separable minimal polynomial over \(K\) (no repeated roots)

questiontext.text: A primitive root modulo \(n\) exists (i.e., \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*\) is cyclic) if and only if \(n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(1,2,4,p^k,\) or \(2p^k\) for odd prime \(p\)

The group \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*\) is cyclic (has a primitive root) for exactly these values of \(n\). For all other \(n\), the group is not cyclic. For prime \(p\): \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*\cong\mathbb{Z}_{p-1}\) is always cyclic.

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is locally compact Hausdorff if and only if it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Homeomorphic to an open subset of a compact Hausdorff space

Equivalently, every point has a neighbourhood basis of compact sets. Examples: \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is locally compact (not compact), any compact Hausdorff space is locally compact. The one-point compactification of a locally compact Hausdorff space is compact Hausdorff.

answer.text: Homeomorphic to an open subset of a compact Hausdorff space

questiontext.text: A short exact sequence \(0\to A\xrightarrow{f} B\xrightarrow{g} C\to 0\) of modules means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f\) is injective, \(g\) is surjective, and \(\ker g=\text{Im}\,f\)

Short exact sequences encode extensions: \(B\) contains \(A\) as a submodule and \(C\cong B/A\). If the sequence splits, \(B\cong A\oplus C\). The Snake Lemma and the Five Lemma are key tools for working with exact sequences, fundamental in homological algebra.

answer.text: \(f\) is injective, \(g\) is surjective, and \(\ker g=\text{Im}\,f\)

questiontext.text: Montel's theorem states: a family \(\mathcal{F}\) of analytic functions on a domain \(D\) is a normal family (every sequence has a locally uniformly convergent subsequence) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally uniformly bounded (uniformly bounded on each compact subset of \(D\))

Montel's theorem is the complex analogue of Arzelà-Ascoli. Normal families are used in the proof of the Riemann Mapping Theorem (compactness argument to extract a conformal bijection) and in the theory of iteration of rational functions (Julia/Fatou sets).

answer.text: Every function in \(\mathcal{F}\) is bounded by 1
\(\mathcal{F}\) is locally uniformly bounded on each compact subset of \(D\)
Every function has the same set of zeros

questiontext.text: Burnside's \(p^aq^b\) theorem states that every group of order \(p^aq^b\) (for primes \(p,q\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Solvable

Burnside's theorem (proved using character theory): groups of order \(p^aq^b\) are solvable. This means they cannot be simple unless they have prime order. The theorem was one of the motivations for the Feit-Thompson theorem (all odd-order groups are solvable), proved in 1963.

questiontext.text: A key property of paracompact spaces is that they admit:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Partitions of unity subordinate to any open cover

A partition of unity is a collection \(\{\phi_\alpha\}\) of continuous functions with \(\text{supp}(\phi_\alpha)\subseteq U_\alpha\) and \(\sum\phi_\alpha=1\). It is used in differential geometry (constructing Riemannian metrics, integration on manifolds), sheaf theory, and differential topology. Every metric space and every compact Hausdorff space is paracompact.

answer.text: Partitions of unity subordinate to any open cover
A unique metric compatible with the topology

questiontext.text: The Schwarz Reflection Principle states: if \(f\) is analytic on the upper half-disk, continuous on the real axis, and real-valued on the real axis, then \(f\) can be extended to the lower half-disk by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(f(\bar z)=\overline{f(z)}\)

The Schwarz Reflection Principle: setting \(f(\bar z)=\overline{f(z)}\) gives an analytic extension across the real axis. This is used to extend conformal maps and to prove symmetry of solutions to Laplace's equation. More generally, one can reflect across any analytic arc.

name.text: Group Theory - Sylow's Third Theorem Application

questiontext.text: A group of order 15 (\(=3\times5\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Cyclic (isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}_{15}\))

By Sylow III: \(n_5\equiv1\pmod5\) and \(n_5\mid3\), so \(n_5=1\). Similarly \(n_3=1\). Both Sylow subgroups are normal. Since \(\gcd(3,5)=1\), \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\times\mathbb{Z}_5\cong\mathbb{Z}_{15}\). More generally, groups of order \(pq\) (\(p

questiontext.text: In a field \(\mathbb{F}_q\) with \(q=p^n\), the Frobenius map \(\phi:x\mapsto x^p\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) A field automorphism of order \(n\)

The Frobenius \(\phi\) is a field automorphism: \(\phi(xy)=x^py^p=\phi(x)\phi(y)\) and \(\phi(x+y)=(x+y)^p=x^p+y^p\) (since \(\binom{p}{k}\equiv0\pmod p\) for \(0

answer.text: A ring homomorphism but not a field automorphism

questiontext.text: The first homology group \(H_1(S^1;\mathbb{Z})\) of the circle is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\mathbb{Z}\)

The simplicial homology: \(H_0(S^1)=\mathbb{Z}\) (one connected component), \(H_1(S^1)=\mathbb{Z}\) (one non-bounding cycle), \(H_k(S^1)=0\) for \(k\geq2\). For the sphere: \(H_k(S^n)=\mathbb{Z}\) for \(k=0,n\) and 0 otherwise. Homology groups are topological invariants capturing "holes" of each dimension.

name.text: Number Theory - Sum of Divisors Function

questiontext.text: For \(n=p_1^{a_1}\cdots p_k^{a_k}\), the sum of divisors function \(\sigma(n)=\sum_{d\mid n}d\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\prod_{i=1}^k\frac{p_i^{a_i+1}-1}{p_i-1}\)

Since \(\sigma\) is multiplicative, \(\sigma(n)=\prod_{i}\sigma(p_i^{a_i})=\prod_i(1+p_i+\cdots+p_i^{a_i})=\prod_i\frac{p_i^{a_i+1}-1}{p_i-1}\). A perfect number satisfies \(\sigma(n)=2n\). Even perfect numbers correspond to Mersenne primes via Euler's theorem: \(n=2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\) where \(2^p-1\) is prime.

questiontext.text: Which of the following maps the upper half-plane \(\text{Im}(z)>0\) conformally onto the open unit disk \(|w|<1\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(w = \frac{z-i}{z+i}\)

The Cayley transform \(w=(z-i)/(z+i)\) maps the upper half-plane to the unit disk, sending \(i\mapsto 0\) and the real axis to the unit circle.

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z)=\frac{\sin z}{z^3}\) has at \(z=0\) a

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) simple pole

Since \(\sin z = z - z^3/6 + \cdots\), we have \(f(z)=z^{-2}-1/6+\cdots\), giving a pole of order 2. Wait — \(\sin z/z^3 = 1/z^2 - 1/6 + z^2/120 - \cdots\), so the pole is of order 2.

questiontext.text: If \(u(x,y)\) is harmonic in a bounded domain and continuous on its closure, then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(u\) attains its maximum and minimum on the boundary

The Maximum Principle for harmonic functions states that a non-constant harmonic function on a bounded domain attains its max and min on the boundary.

answer.text: \(u\) attains its maximum and minimum on the boundary
\(u\) attains its maximum in the interior

questiontext.text: The number of generators of the cyclic group \(\mathbb{Z}_{36}\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 12

The number of generators of \(\mathbb{Z}_n\) is \(\phi(n)\). Here \(\phi(36)=36(1-1/2)(1-1/3)=12\).

questiontext.text: The automorphism group \(\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_p)\) for a prime \(p\) is isomorphic to

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\mathbb{Z}_{p-1}\)

\(\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_n)\cong(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times\). For prime \(p\), \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\) is cyclic of order \(p-1\), hence \(\cong\mathbb{Z}_{p-1}\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following polynomials is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(x^4+x+1\) (checking over \(\mathbb{F}_2\) shows no roots and no degree-2 factor)

Actually \(x^4+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) — it has no rational roots and does not factor as a product of two quadratics over \(\mathbb{Q}\). Wait: \(x^4+1=(x^2+\sqrt{2}x+1)(x^2-\sqrt{2}x+1)\) over \(\mathbb{R}\) but not over \(\mathbb{Q}\). Indeed \(x^4+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

questiontext.text: The degree \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 4

\([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2\) and \(\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\) (since \(\sqrt{3}=a+b\sqrt{2}\) leads to a contradiction), so \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})]=2\), giving total degree 4.

questiontext.text: Which of the following subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \([0,1]\)

A subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected if and only if it is an interval (including single points). \([0,1]\) is an interval, hence connected. \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\), and \(\{0\}\cup\{1\}\) are disconnected.

questiontext.text: A continuous image of a compact space is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) compact

If \(f:X\to Y\) is continuous and \(X\) is compact, then \(f(X)\) is compact. This is a fundamental theorem in topology.

questiontext.text: In the symmetric group \(S_4\), the maximum order of any element is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 4

The order of a permutation equals the lcm of its cycle lengths. In \(S_4\), cycle types and their orders: (1,1,1,1)→1, (2,1,1)→2, (2,2)→2, (3,1)→3, (4)→4. Maximum is 4 (from a 4-cycle).

questiontext.text: Jensen's formula relates the modulus of an analytic function to its

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) zeros inside a disk

Jensen's formula: if \(f\) is analytic in \(|z|\leq r\) with zeros \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) (counted with multiplicity) inside \(|z|

answer.text: Zeros inside a disk and boundary values

questiontext.text: Every finite integral domain is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) a field

In a finite integral domain \(R\), for any nonzero \(a\), the map \(x\mapsto ax\) is injective (no zero divisors) hence bijective (finiteness), so \(a\) has a multiplicative inverse. Thus \(R\) is a field.

answer.text: A PID but not necessarily a field
A UFD but not necessarily a PID
A Noetherian ring but not necessarily an integral domain

questiontext.text: A metric space \((X,d)\) is called complete if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) every Cauchy sequence converges in \(X\)

Completeness means every Cauchy sequence (a sequence where \(d(x_m,x_n)\to 0\) as \(m,n\to\infty\)) converges to a point in \(X\).

answer.text: Every sequence has a convergent subsequence

questiontext.text: Which group is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Klein four-group \(V_4\)

The Klein four-group \(V_4=\{e,a,b,ab\}\) with every non-identity element of order 2 is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_2\). It is not cyclic (unlike \(\mathbb{Z}_4\)).

questiontext.text: The number of elements in any finite field must be

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) a prime power \(p^n\)

By the classification of finite fields, every finite field has exactly \(p^n\) elements for some prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\). For each prime power there is a unique (up to isomorphism) finite field \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\).

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z)=\frac{e^z}{(z-1)^2}\) at \(z=1\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(e\)

For a pole of order 2 at \(z=1\): \(\text{Res}_{z=1}f(z)=\lim_{z\to 1}\frac{d}{dz}[(z-1)^2f(z)]=\frac{d}{dz}[e^z]\big|_{z=1}=e^1=e\).

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff (\(T_2\)) if and only if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods

The Hausdorff condition (\(T_2\)) requires that for any two distinct points \(x eq y\), there exist open sets \(U i x\) and \(V i y\) with \(U\cap V=\emptyset\). This ensures limits of sequences are unique.

answer.text: Distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods
Every sequence has at most one limit point
